payload.input: The doorbell chimes at eleven-fifty-eight, those three descending notes that sound like a sigh. Maya rises from the couch, but when she turns, Ethan is already standing in the hallway archway—transformed. He wears the charcoal trousers he saves for Easter service, pressed sharp as knives, paired with a crisp white oxford shirt buttoned to the collar. His sandy hair, usually sticking up in cowlicks, has been tamed with the pomade their aunt sent last Christmas, the expensive stuff that smells like sandalwood and tobacco, slicked back from his forehead in a wave that makes him look suddenly, jarringly older. His face is scrubbed pink, jawline sharp, eyes clear and wide with a mixture of terror and determination. "You look..." Maya starts, her voice catching. "Like someone worth investing in," Ethan finishes, his voice steadier than it has been in days. The chime sounds again, insistent. Through the peephole, Vivian stands with her camel coat and swan neck, Julian beside her with his reflective sunglasses and leather camera bag. But behind them, parked at the curb, Maya sees another vehicle—a silver Lexus with tinted windows, engine running, ready for extraction. Maya unlocks the door, pulling it open just as Vivian raises her hand to knock a third time. The woman’s eyes—hazel, intelligent, calculating—sweep over Maya’s bare legs and underwear, then flick to Ethan with an approving smile that shows too many teeth. "Precious," Vivian murmurs, her voice carrying the same cultured warmth from the video message. "But we have a scheduling conflict. Your shepherd is approaching from the east, three minutes out by my estimate. We passed him at the stoplight, Bible on the passenger seat, looking determined."  Julian checks his watch—a vintage piece with a leather strap. "Eleven-fifty-nine. We need to be gone before he turns onto the cul-de-sac."  Ethan steps forward, his polished shoes clicking against the tile.
